blueprint_refs: poi_topk_recall: "recall@K of ground-truth POIs appearing in the simulated day (set-based), average across days. K is fixed (e.g., 5 or length of simulated sequence, designed)"

evidence.metrics: topk_poi_recall mean = 0.0 on validation; also 0.0 for every calibration iter

reflection.error_identification: POI recall is exactly 0.0 across all calibration iterations and the held-out evaluation, indicating the metric implementation is likely wrong (or always comparing disjoint sets).
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In Evaluator.compute_metrics(), recall is computed as intersection(gt_set, sim_pois_k)/len(gt_set). This is not recall@K as defined: denominator should be |GT| but numerator should compare to a simulated *set* of size K (or all simulated if shorter). Additionally, using the first K simulated visits (ordered) plus dividing by full GT set can drive values toward 0 when GT sizes are typically >K; the blueprint allows K to be 5 or length of simulated sequence, but denominator must be min(K, |GT|) if using recall@K or remain |GT| if using coverage recall but then K should not truncate the simulated set. Current hybrid makes the metric overly harsh and can appear degenerate when sequences are longer than K.
reflection.correct_approach:
Implement recall@K correctly and deterministically:
- Let sim_set = set(sim_pois[:K]).
- Let gt_set = set(gt_pois).
- Define recall_at_k = |gt_set ∩ sim_set| / min(len(gt_set), K) (standard recall@K), OR if you want "coverage of GT by top-K simulated" then keep denominator len(gt_set) but do not call it recall@K and expect small values.
Also handle cases where sim has <K by using min(K, len(sim)).
reflection.key_insight: When a metric is used as a calibration signal, implement it exactly per definition; a subtly mis-specified denominator/truncation can collapse the signal (here: always near 0), making calibration chase the wrong objective.

blueprint_refs: TrajectoryStringFormatter.FORMAT_SPEC: "comma+space separators and ends with a single '.'"

evidence.metrics: Sample outputs show '; ' separators and no trailing '.' (e.g., "... at 22:11:18" without period).

reflection.error_identification: Serialized trajectories do not follow the declared format_spec: code emits semicolon-separated visits and omits the required trailing period, while format_spec claims comma+space and trailing '.'. This can break downstream parsers/scorers and invalidates output contract consistency.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: TrajectoryStringFormatter documents a canonical comma-separated, period-terminated format, but format_day_string() returns f"{prefix} " + "; ".join(parts) and never appends a period. The parser parse_1921y_records() also expects semicolons, creating an internal inconsistency with FORMAT_SPEC.
reflection.correct_approach:
Make formatter and parser consistent with the canonical spec:
- Update format_day_string() to use ", " separators and append ".".
- Update parse_1921y_records() splitting logic to accept commas (and optionally semicolons for robustness) but standardize emission.
- Ensure TrajectoryStringFormatter.FORMAT_SPEC matches actual emission.
reflection.key_insight: Never let the declared format_spec diverge from emitted strings; keep a single canonical serializer and have parsers accept supersets for robustness.

blueprint_refs: simulation_evaluation.metrics: stop_count_mae, category_mix_jsd, time_of_day_emd, poi_topk_recall, radius_of_gyration_error

evidence.metrics: Reported metrics are category_share_mae, tod_jsd_avg, trip_distance_wasserstein, transition_divergence, stop_count_kl, etc., which do not match blueprint names/definitions.

reflection.error_identification: The simulator is evaluated and calibrated on a different metric suite than required by the blueprint (e.g., uses JSD over time bins instead of time_of_day_emd; uses share MAE instead of category_mix_jsd; missing radius_of_gyration_error). This makes reported performance incomparable to spec and can mis-tune parameters.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator.compute_metrics() implements category_share_mae and tod_jsd_avg and trip_distance_wasserstein, but blueprint requires category_mix_jsd and time_of_day_emd and radius_of_gyration_error. There is no implementation of radius of gyration; time metric is JSD not EMD; category metric is MAE not JSD. Calibration objective weights also target these non-blueprint metrics.
reflection.correct_approach:
Align evaluation to blueprint:
- Replace category_share_mae with category_mix_jsd using js_divergence() over (fine or super) category distribution.
- Replace tod_jsd_avg with time_of_day_emd by computing minutes-of-day samples or histograms and using wasserstein_1d() as an EMD proxy over binned distributions.
- Implement radius_of_gyration_error: compute daily radius of gyration for simulated and GT using POI lat/lon; exclude visits with missing coords (as blueprint allows) and aggregate absolute/relative error.
- Keep extra diagnostics metrics if desired, but report and optimize the blueprint-required ones.
reflection.key_insight: Calibration optimizes exactly what you measure; if your metric suite drifts from spec, you’ll tune the wrong behavior even if the simulator ‘looks good’ on internal metrics.

blueprint_refs: baseline_category_transition_model: "fit Markov transition probabilities ..."

evidence.metrics: transition_divergence ~= 0.6931471803 (≈ ln(2)) consistently across calibration and evaluation

reflection.error_identification: Transition divergence is pegged at ~ln(2), a common maximum for JSD between two highly distinct distributions, suggesting simulated vs GT transition bigram distributions share little overlap or the computation is collapsing to a two-point support.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Two likely contributing mechanisms in code: (1) The simulator generates super-category sequences (sc_seq) and then selects POIs by supercat; if GT transitions are computed over fine categories or have different supercat mapping, overlap can be low. (2) In Evaluator.compute_metrics(), if either gt_bigram or sim_bigram is empty it falls back to {"None":1.0}; but here value is ln(2) not 0, implying both are non-empty but possibly dominated by disjoint keys due to inconsistent category->supercat mapping or because simulation uses a restricted tuned_supercats subset while GT uses full supercat space.
reflection.correct_approach:
Disambiguate then fix:
- Add diagnostics: number of unique bigrams in GT vs SIM; top-20 bigrams and overlaps.
- Ensure both GT and SIM transitions are computed on the same taxonomy (either fine categories or super-categories per catto.json).
- Avoid restricting the transition support to tuned_supercats during simulation; keep full supercat support from dataset.poi_tokens_by_supercat keys + 'Unknown' so simulated bigrams can match GT support.
- Consider adding per-user smoothing rather than hard global fallback to reduce mode collapse.
reflection.key_insight: When a divergence metric saturates at a constant across parameter sweeps, treat it as a taxonomy/support mismatch or a collapsed feature extractor; add overlap diagnostics and ensure identical representation for GT vs SIM.

blueprint_refs: radius_of_gyration_error: "requires POI lat/lon"; "handle missing POIs by excluding those visits"
reflection.error_identification: Ground-truth step distances are never computed (gt_total_steps=0) on the evaluated split, making any distance-based comparison unreliable and indicating GT POIs may not join to coordinates or GT sequences are too short after parsing.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator.add_step_dist() increments gt_total_steps for i in range(1,len(gt)), so gt_total_steps=0 implies almost all GT days have <=1 visit after parsing, or POI tokens in GT do not match dataset.poi_by_token keys (so visits exist but steps not counted would still increment totals—however totals increment before coord check, so this points to len(gt)<=1). That suggests parse_1921y_records() may be splitting incorrectly for the dataset delimiter (expects ';'), causing it to treat multi-visit strings as a single token or fail to parse additional visits.
reflection.correct_approach:
Make parsing robust to the true delimiter(s) in 1921Y.json:
- Inspect raw day strings to confirm whether visits are separated by ';' or ','.
- Update parse_1921y_records() to split on both ';' and ',' (careful to not split inside categories/POI names) or use a regex to extract repeated "<loc> at <time>" patterns.
- After fix, assert per-day visit counts distribution for GT has reasonable mean >1 and gt_total_steps>0 in diagnostics.
Also implement radius_of_gyration_error per blueprint once coordinates join is working.
reflection.key_insight: If GT-derived trajectory stats (like step counts) are zero, debug parsing/joining before tuning the generator; otherwise all spatial metrics become meaningless.

blueprint_refs: outputs: ["calibrated_parameters", "evaluation_results_on_validation"]

evidence.metrics: SIMULATION RESULTS show calibrated_parameters.best_objective_on_training/best_params, while code writes best_objective/best_parameters in calibrated_parameters.json

reflection.error_identification: The on-disk output structure appears inconsistent with what the run summary expects (results loader reports best_objective_on_training/best_params, but code writes best_objective and best_parameters). This risks downstream tooling failing to read results consistently.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In main(), calibrated_parameters dict uses keys "best_parameters" and "best_objective". However, SIMULATION RESULTS.calibrated_parameters shows different keys ("best_params", "best_objective_on_training"), implying an external evaluator/loader expects those names or the saved file differs from the code’s intent.
reflection.correct_approach:
Stabilize output schema by matching the actually consumed keys:
- In calibrated_parameters.json, include both naming variants (aliases) to be safe: write best_params and best_parameters pointing to same object; best_objective_on_training and best_objective same value.
- Keep a single canonical set in future, but don’t break existing loaders.
reflection.key_insight: When outputs are consumed by external evaluators, treat key names as API contracts; add backward-compatible aliases rather than renaming fields outright.

blueprint_refs: baseline_poi_choice_model: mixture of (a) empirical POI popularity ... and (b) distance-decay ...
poi_topk_recall definition
reflection.error_identification: POI top-k recall is essentially zero, meaning simulated days almost never include any ground-truth POIs even as a set match.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: POI sampling within a super-category uses per-user POI preference weights (baseline.poi_pref) plus distance, but preference mass is extremely sparse (only on POIs seen in 2019–2020). With w_pref often high (best params ~0.94), the sampler concentrates on a small set of historically seen POIs, which in 2021 often differs from the user’s actual POIs; simultaneously, candidates per supercat are huge (all POIs in that supercat), and distance decay is computed from a context POI that may not be aligned to the day’s actual starting anchor, further reducing overlap with ground truth. Net: almost no exact token matches.
reflection.correct_approach:
Make the POI-choice model less brittle and more user-anchored:
1) In Resident.simulate_day, define candidates as a union of (a) supercat candidates and (b) user-specific top-M POIs within that supercat (from baseline counts) plus (c) global top-M POIs within that supercat; then sample from this reduced candidate set.
2) Smooth poi_pref within a supercat: if a candidate is unseen by the user, give it a small prior proportional to global popularity (already computed in build_network_and_agents as global_poi_counts; reuse by exposing a per-supercat global popularity table rather than only a global list).
3) Lower default w_pref (e.g., 0.3–0.7) and widen calibration search; current best pushes it to ~0.94, reinforcing sparsity.
4) Ensure a consistent “start anchor” for distance: if ctx_poi exists but is not in the POI catalog / lacks coords, fall back to a home/anchor POI with coords.
reflection.key_insight: When the evaluation requires exact POI token matches, any purely “user-history-only” POI preference will collapse recall under distribution shift; always include global/popularity smoothing and candidate-set pruning.

blueprint_refs: simulation_evaluation.metrics includes radius_of_gyration_error
definition: error between simulated and GT daily radius of gyration computed from POI coordinates

evidence.metrics: No radius_of_gyration_error reported; instead trip_distance_wasserstein is used

reflection.error_identification: Blueprint requires radius_of_gyration_error, but the implementation reports trip_distance_wasserstein and never computes radius of gyration.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator.compute_metrics computes step-distance Wasserstein (trip_distance_wasserstein) based on consecutive-visit distances, but does not compute per-day radius of gyration from POI coordinates nor compare to GT as specified.
reflection.correct_approach:
Replace or add the blueprint metric:
- For each (user, day), map each visited POI to (lat, lon); drop visits with missing coords (as blueprint allows).
- Compute center of mass and radius of gyration r_g = sqrt(mean(||x_i - x_cm||^2)).
- Compute absolute error |r_g_sim - r_g_gt| (or relative if desired; blueprint allows absolute/relative). Aggregate mean over days.
- Emit as radius_of_gyration_error and (if keeping trip_distance_wasserstein for diagnostics) mark it non-objective or extra.
reflection.key_insight: Don’t silently substitute evaluation metrics: implement blueprint metrics directly, and only add extras as explicitly non-contract diagnostics.

blueprint_refs: time_of_day_emd definition: Earth Mover's Distance over minutes-of-day
reflection.error_identification: The simulator evaluates time-of-day distribution mismatch using JSD over coarse 30-min bins rather than the blueprint-required EMD over minutes-of-day.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator.compute_metrics builds weekday/weekend histograms on 30-min bins and computes js_divergence, outputting tod_jsd_avg. This deviates from the blueprint’s Earth Mover’s Distance requirement, and may hide systematic shifts (EMD is sensitive to “how far” mass moved).
reflection.correct_approach:
Implement EMD (1D Wasserstein) on minute-of-day:
- Collect all visit minutes-of-day for GT and sim across the split (or per day then average).
- Compute wasserstein_1d on the raw minute lists (already implemented) or on cumulative distributions.
- Either replace tod_jsd_avg with time_of_day_emd or output both and align the objective to blueprint time_of_day_emd.
reflection.key_insight: For circular/ordered domains like time-of-day, prefer distance-aware metrics (EMD/Wasserstein) over divergence on coarse bins when the spec demands shift magnitude.

blueprint_refs: category_mix_jsd definition: JSD between simulated vs ground-truth distributions of fine categories (or super-categories)
reflection.error_identification: The implementation reports category_share_mae (mean absolute share difference) on super-categories, not JSD on categories/super-categories as specified.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator.compute_metrics maps each visit to a super-category and uses shares_mae; it never computes Jensen–Shannon divergence over fine categories or over super-categories.
reflection.correct_approach:
Compute category_mix_jsd per blueprint:
- Decide whether to use fine category or super-category consistently (blueprint allows either; pick super-category if catto coverage is partial).
- Build normalized distributions for GT and sim across the split.
- Compute js_divergence and output as category_mix_jsd. Keep category_share_mae only as an auxiliary diagnostic if desired.
reflection.key_insight: Match the metric family in the spec: MAE on shares and JSD capture different failures; using the wrong one can miscalibrate the shift parameters.

blueprint_refs: stop_count_mae definition
theta_stop_count_multiplier calibratable parameter

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=2.2225; diagnostics gt_mean_visits_per_day=7.759 vs sim_mean_visits_per_day=6.686; stop_count_kl=0.4452

reflection.error_identification: Stop counts are materially off: simulations average ~1.07 fewer visits/day than ground truth, with large per-day absolute error (~2.22) and distribution mismatch (KL~0.45).
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Stop-count generation rescales a discrete pmf by rounding counts (c2 = round(c*mult)) and then applies ad-hoc neighbor smoothing (adding mass to c±1). This can distort the original distribution (multi-modal shapes collapse), and the later truncation in simulate_day (break if t_min >= day) further reduces realized stop count, biasing downward vs the sampled stop_count.
reflection.correct_approach:
Stabilize stop-count control and prevent truncation bias:
1) Sample stop_count directly from the baseline pmf, then apply multiplier by shifting probabilities rather than rounding counts (e.g., reweight each count c by exp(-|c - c*mult|/tau) and renormalize).
2) Track when the time loop truncates (t_min>=MINUTES_PER_DAY) and either (a) cap gap sampling to fit remaining day time, or (b) allow later-day timestamps up to 23:59 by compressing gaps.
3) Add a diagnostic: sampled_stop_count vs realized_len(visits) to quantify truncation-induced error per split.
reflection.key_insight: If you sample a target sequence length but later time-feasibility truncates the sequence, you must either enforce feasibility during length sampling or adjust gaps to honor the sampled length; otherwise stop-count metrics will be systematically biased.

blueprint_refs: radius_of_gyration_error requires POI lat/lon join; handle missing POIs by excluding those visits
POI catalog join by matching POI identifier strings
reflection.error_identification: About 70% of ground-truth step distances cannot be computed due to missing coordinates, which makes distance-based evaluation noisy and potentially misleading.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: The POI coordinate join is token-exact: dataset visit tokens must match poi_category_... tokens exactly. Any formatting differences (extra whitespace, punctuation, category name variations, casing) will cause a miss and be treated as missing coords. The simulator’s generated POIs all come from the POI catalog, so sim missingness is 0, creating an asymmetry.
reflection.correct_approach:
Improve token normalization at parse/join time without inventing new files:
- In parse_1921y_records, normalize loc token by stripping trailing punctuation and collapsing internal whitespace.
- When loading poi_catalog, also store a normalized-key index (e.g., strip and collapse whitespace) to map GT tokens to POIInfo when exact match fails.
- Log top-N unmatched GT tokens to confirm whether misses are due to formatting vs truly absent POIs.
reflection.key_insight: Distance-based metrics are only as good as the POI-token join; always implement robust normalization and log unmatched identifiers before trusting mobility-radius results.

blueprint_refs: Online OOD context window rule: construct a 7-day context window using only days strictly earlier than target; may backfill from late 2020

evidence.metrics: No direct metric; but affects realism and downstream recall/distance plausibility

reflection.error_identification: If no visits exist in the strict 7-day window, the code backfills from any earlier day (potentially far earlier than late 2020), which can leak stale context and violate the intended 7-day rule semantics.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: MobilitySimulator._context_last_poi first tries [target-7d, target), but on empty it falls back to any r.date < target_date without limiting how far back it goes. Blueprint permits backfill from late 2020, not arbitrary history.
reflection.correct_approach:
Constrain the fallback:
- If the 7-day window is empty, extend the window backwards only to a bounded horizon (e.g., 30 days) and allow crossing year boundary into late 2020, but do not use arbitrarily old records.
- Alternatively, if no context in bounded window, fall back to anchor/home POI rather than ancient last visit.
reflection.key_insight: Context windows in OOD simulations must be time-bounded even in fallback paths; otherwise you silently change the conditioning regime and may inflate/deflate metrics unpredictably.

blueprint_refs: outputs: calibrated_parameters, evaluation_results_on_validation
holdout_plan: V_calib (first 80%) and V_test (last 20%)
comparison_method: evaluate on validation for calibration, then report held-out test once

evidence.metrics: evaluation_results_on_validation.json contains split=per_user_last_20pct_of_2021 and note says held-out V_test

reflection.error_identification: The program writes evaluation results on the per-user last-20% (test split) into a file named evaluation_results_on_validation.json, which is semantically confusing versus the blueprint naming.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In main(), after calibrating on V_calib, it rolls out on splits.test_days_by_user but stores it under evaluation_results_on_validation and simulated_trajectories_validation.
reflection.correct_approach:
Align naming to split semantics while respecting required output filenames:
- Keep required filename evaluation_results_on_validation.json if mandated by external contract, but include explicit fields: {"evaluated_split": "V_test"} and optionally also write a separate evaluation_results_on_test.json if allowed by the environment (only if not prohibited).
- Alternatively, evaluate on V_calib and store that as “on_validation”, and store V_test metrics under calibrated_parameters.meta or another required artifact, ensuring no tuning uses V_test.
reflection.key_insight: When output filenames are fixed by contract, make the evaluated split unambiguous in the JSON payload to prevent accidental ‘validation vs test’ misuse downstream.
Keep generated code tightly aligned to the simulation blueprint; delete unrelated agent pipelines to avoid scope drift and accidental dependency/runtime issues.
Calibration is only as good as the metric contract: match blueprint metric names/definitions exactly or you will optimize the wrong thing.
For discrete count targets, scaling a sampled count is a weak surrogate; model the count distribution directly (and condition on covariates like day-of-week) to reduce both mean error and KL.
Never truncate candidate sets with an arbitrary fixed prefix; candidate generation must preserve high-probability items under the agent’s preference/transition model or you cap achievable recall.
Sparse POI-to-POI transitions need a principled backoff (to category transitions or preferences); using a near-zero fallback silently disables the feature and degrades downstream choice quality.

Time realism improves when you model forward-looking time increments (gaps) rather than sampling absolute target times and snapping; always expose per-segment diagnostics (per-category JSD) to localize timing failures.
If the spec includes an exogenous covariate (day-of-week), it must be threaded through parsing → priors → simulation; otherwise you average over regimes and degrade realism.

Never sample a fixed plan length if the simulator can silently truncate; either (a) enforce feasibility before committing events or (b) model termination explicitly so calibration can control the events/day distribution.
When a metric is defined on ‘start times’, don’t evaluate ‘arrival after travel/dwell’—align the simulator’s emitted timestamps and evaluator to the same semantic definition.
Simulation entrypoints must be import-safe; always use an __main__ guard so orchestration is explicit and testable.
If you calibrate on a subset, it must preserve the validation distribution of the metrics you optimize (stratify or average across resamples), otherwise selection will not transfer.

Candidate generation should be preference-preserving by construction (always include top preferred items), and exploration should be explicit and parameterized—not an implicit side effect of random candidate pools.
Separate “string-level format validity” from “file/schema validity”: validate and emit the exact top-level JSON schema required by the dataset, not an enriched wrapper.
Any utility term mixing “duration” and “absolute time-of-day” is a red flag—ensure units match (arrival_time vs target_time) or calibration will mask the bug by truncation artifacts.
If you already have a stop-count model, don’t pre-truncate it with a global average-time heuristic; enforce feasibility during sequential generation using realized travel+dwell.
Coordinate-missing handling affects both behavior (via utility) and evaluation (via dropped steps). Always measure missingness and use a data-derived fallback, not a hardcoded constant.
Absolute time-of-day priors are not sufficient for categories that are transition-driven; model gaps/relative timing and use absolute histograms as a soft constraint.
If dwell is learned from inter-event gaps, you must not also add full travel time on top—otherwise the day budget collapses and stop counts truncate.
Evaluation smoothing should prevent zeros without flattening distributions; large pseudocounts can hide errors and mislead calibration.
Missing-coordinate handling must be consistent between generation and evaluation; otherwise you optimize against a shifted support.
Aggregate time-of-day metrics can look acceptable while individual trajectories are implausible; add simple constraints (active window, repeat penalty) to stabilize micro-dynamics.
When user feedback is absent, proactively emit interpretable diagnostics that directly map to blueprint metrics to accelerate the next iteration.

Strict schema tasks require the primary artifact to match the reference file structure exactly—wrapping metadata breaks downstream consumers even if strings are valid. Treat schema-compatibility as a first-class contract: produce a dedicated ‘ground-truth-shaped’ artifact, and put diagnostics in separate files.
Keep simulation code minimal and blueprint-scoped; unrelated integrations should live behind separate entrypoints to avoid accidental coupling.
Distance-based metrics are only meaningful when coordinate coverage is comparable; always quantify missingness and gate calibration/interpretation accordingly.

If time-of-day is an evaluation target, avoid layered heuristics (clamps/late penalties) that silently re-shape the sampled time distribution; keep timing as a primary sampled variable with feasibility handled by resampling.
When the item universe is huge, don’t smooth over the full global support; define a principled candidate/support set so preference learning remains identifiable and recall doesn’t collapse.
Don’t reuse ‘smoothing’ as a behavioral mixture control; separate statistical smoothing from decision-policy mixing to keep calibration interpretable.

If output format allows non-decreasing times but realism/metrics need separation, always enforce a minimum increment (e.g., +1 minute) between successive events and terminate the day when the schedule cannot remain feasible—never ‘clamp’ multiple events to the same boundary minute.
Don’t infer dwell from start-to-start gaps unless you explicitly model travel and handle zero-gap artifacts; otherwise you’ll miscalibrate both stop counts and time-of-day placement.
A single global time-of-day weight is rarely sufficient; use adaptive backoff based on category data volume and treat ‘home’ as a special anchor process instead of a normal category histogram.
Personalization metrics degrade when exploration is added as a broad tail; keep exploration as a small, explicit budget and never flatten preference rankings with uniform bonuses.

When a metric drops missing values asymmetrically between GT and SIM, you must align supports (or condition evaluation on comparable subsets), otherwise the metric can be ‘good’ while the behavior is not.
Simulation pipelines should be import-safe; always guard top-level execution so components can be reused, tested, and instrumented without triggering a full run.
